chain_of_thought: Step 1 — Identify the first application-level native frame below allocators/sanitizers. The process terminated at stream_gets in libmainstone.so. This is not a libc allocator frame; it is an application-internal helper. The frame above it is ms_decode_buf_pnm, also in libmainstone.so. MsDecodeBuffer is the highest app-owned frame present in the LibMap and is the first decompilation target.

Step 2 — Ghidra MCP decompilation. Searching for MsDecodeBuffer in libmainstone.so @ 0x00682ef0 yields a thunk: (*(code *)PTR_MsDecodeBuffer_00682ef0)(). Per the exploration rules, since this is a wrapper I must decompile its caller. The caller is Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData in libbcr.so. Decompilation shows: uVar2 = (**(code **)(*(long *)param_1 + 0x558))(param_1, param_3) — GetArrayLength on the Java byte[]; uVar4 = (**(code **)(*(long *)param_1 + 0x5c0))(param_1, param_3, 0) — GetPrimitiveArrayCritical returning a raw pointer; iVar3 = MsDecodeBuffer(uVar4, uVar2, 4, &local_60) — passes raw pointer and length with format discriminator 4 and no prior validation.

Step 3 — Backward data-flow for all three MsDecodeBuffer arguments. src (uVar4): raw pointer into the Java byte[], attacker-controlled byte-for-byte with no copy or transformation. len (uVar2): Java array length retrieved verbatim from the JVM, attacker-controlled. format (4): compile-time constant, not attacker-influenced. dst (&local_60): stack-allocated output _MsImage* structure, not directly attacker-controlled. Tracing further up: Java RecognizeCard(byte[] bArr, int i6) allocates a ResultCard and calls RecognizeCardData immediately — no length clamping, no header check, no encoding validation at any point in the Java call graph.

Step 4 — JNI and Java analysis via Jadx. The three Java call graph paths (nd.k::run, camcard.d::doInBackground, ob.g2::run) all terminate at RecognizeCardData without any intermediate validation. In a real app, bArr originates from camera frame capture, gallery image selection, or an externally imported file — all realistic attacker-reachable sources. The int mode parameter is also caller-supplied and not bounded.

Step 5 — Function pointer resolution for PTR_MsDecodeBuffer_00682ef0. The pointer is runtime-resolved and the dispatch target cannot be confirmed statically. However, the stack trace makes the resolved path explicit: MsDecodeBuffer dispatches to ms_decode_buf_pnm (consistent with format discriminator 4 selecting a PNM decoder), which then calls stream_gets. stream_gets is an internal line-oriented reader operating on a memory-backed stream. It could not be decompiled ('??' in the stack), but its name and position are consistent with an unsafe fgets-style helper that reads until newline or NUL without enforcing the original buffer end as a limit.

Step 6 — Classification. The unbroken taint chain is: attacker byte[] → JNI (no validation) → MsDecodeBuffer → ms_decode_buf_pnm → stream_gets (crash). This is a classic image-parser memory-safety bug. stream_gets is not an allocator sentinel or harness stub; it is the application's own parsing helper crashing on malformed input. CWE-787 (Out-of-Bounds Write) and CWE-119 (Buffer Restriction Failure) apply. Confidence is 0.78 rather than 0.9+ because ms_decode_buf_pnm and stream_gets could not be decompiled, so the precise overflow type (stack vs heap, read vs write) is inferred from naming and call context.
reasons: Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData retrieves the raw byte[] pointer and array length directly from the JVM and forwards them to MsDecodeBuffer without any bounds check, header validation, or size clamping.

MsDecodeBuffer dispatches via function pointer to ms_decode_buf_pnm (format type 4), which calls stream_gets — an internal line-reader operating on the attacker-controlled buffer with no confirmed boundary enforcement.

The crash site (stream_gets in libmainstone.so) is an application-internal parsing helper, not a harness artifact or allocator sentinel; the taint path from attacker-supplied image bytes to the crash is unbroken and reachable via camera/gallery input in production.

evidence.note: JNI retrieves raw byte[] pointer (uVar4) and array length (uVar2) verbatim from the JVM and passes both to MsDecodeBuffer with format discriminator 4 and no prior validation. This is the entry point of the unbroken taint chain.

Public Java API passes arbitrary byte[] directly to native RecognizeCardData with no length cap, format check, or content validation. Three async paths in the call graph (nd.k::run, camcard.d::doInBackground, ob.g2::run) reach this method from user-initiated operations.

Dispatch thunk with no app-level bounds logic; runtime-resolved pointer dispatches to ms_decode_buf_pnm for format type 4, which calls stream_gets where the crash occurs. All validation responsibility falls on the resolved implementation.

recommendations: Decompile ms_decode_buf_pnm and stream_gets in libmainstone.so to identify the exact read loop and add an explicit remaining-bytes limit derived from the original buffer length before each line read.

In Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData, enforce a maximum buffer size and validate a minimal image header (magic bytes, declared dimensions) before invoking MsDecodeBuffer; reject inputs that cannot conform to supported formats.

Replace stream_gets with a bounds-aware alternative (e.g., a custom fgets-equivalent that accepts an explicit limit derived from the allocated buffer size) to prevent reads past the end of the attacker-supplied byte[].
assumptions: stream_gets is an internal line-reader operating on a memory-backed stream without enforcing the original buffer boundary as a hard read limit.
The Java byte[] supplied to RecognizeCard originates from camera frames, gallery images, or imported files — all realistic attacker-reachable sources.
Format discriminator value 4 consistently routes MsDecodeBuffer to ms_decode_buf_pnm in production, matching the observed crash path.
limitations: stream_gets and ms_decode_buf_pnm could not be decompiled; the precise overflow primitive (stack vs heap, OOB-read vs OOB-write) is inferred from naming and call context rather than confirmed from code.
The implementation behind PTR_MsDecodeBuffer_00682ef0 is runtime-resolved; static analysis confirms only the dispatch thunk.
No SIGSEGV fault address or sanitizer report is available, preventing precise classification of the crash as a read or write violation.

exploit.trigger_method: Crafted PNM-format (or format-type-4) image byte sequence delivered to BCREngine.RecognizeCard(byte[], int)
prerequisites: Attacker can supply image data processed by the app (camera capture, gallery import, shared file via intent, or any IPC path reaching RecognizeCard).
Target device runs the vulnerable version of libmainstone.so and libbcr.so loaded by com.intsig.BCRLite.
No out-of-band length or content sanitization is applied to the byte[] before it reaches the native layer.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malformed PNM-format image whose header or line data causes stream_gets to read beyond the allocated buffer, achieving an OOB-read or OOB-write primitive.
Deliver the crafted image to CamCard via a realistic channel: share the file to the app, trigger import from gallery, or invoke the scan activity with a file URI pointing to the payload.
The app calls BCREngine.RecognizeCard(byte[], int), which forwards the raw byte[] to Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData without validation.
MsDecodeBuffer dispatches to ms_decode_buf_pnm → stream_gets; the malformed input triggers memory corruption at stream_gets.
Depending on heap/stack layout at the time of corruption, the primitive may be escalated toward code execution; at minimum, a reliable app crash (DoS) is achievable.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.intsig.BCRLite/<ScanActivity> --es image_path "/sdcard/Download/payload_card.pnm"
exploit.notes: The precise PNM structure that triggers the crash must be refined through targeted fuzzing of RecognizeCardData(byte[], ResultCard, int) with format discriminator 4. Activity name is a placeholder pending AndroidManifest review. Exploitability beyond DoS is theoretical until the OOB primitive type is confirmed via dynamic analysis.

chain_of_thought: Step 1 — Identify the first application-level native frame. The process terminated inside art::mirror::Class::GetDescriptor, which is a deep ART runtime frame. Moving up, the first app-owned frame is recognize in libbcr.so, called directly from Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData. Both must be decompiled.

Step 2 — Ghidra MCP decompilation. recognize in libbcr.so decompiles to a pure dispatch thunk: void recognize(_JNIEnv*, _jclass*, _MsImage*, _jobject*, int) { (*(code *)PTR_recognize_00682c08)(); return; }. Per the mandatory rules, since this is a thunk I decompile the caller. Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData decompiles to: GetArrayLength and GetPrimitiveArrayCritical on the Java byte[]; MsDecodeBuffer(uVar4, uVar2, 4, &local_60); ReleasePrimitiveArrayCritical; then if (iVar3 == 0) { uVar2 = recognize(param_1, param_2, local_60, param_4, param_5); MsFreeImage(local_60); }. The _MsImage* produced by MsDecodeBuffer (local_60) is passed directly into recognize without any field-level validation.

Step 3 — Backward data-flow for recognize arguments. _MsImage* (local_60): output of MsDecodeBuffer which processes an attacker-controlled byte[] with no validation. If MsDecodeBuffer mis-parses the input, local_60 can contain attacker-influenced field values such as corrupted dimension values, data pointers, or internal string references. _JNIEnv* (param_1): the JNI environment, not attacker-controlled. _jobject* resultCard (param_4): the Java ResultCard object. int mode (param_5): the int flag from RecognizeCard, attacker-supplied. No validation of any local_60 fields (width, height, data pointer, stride, internal string pointers) is performed between MsDecodeBuffer's return and the recognize call.

Step 4 — JNI and Java analysis via Jadx. RecognizeCard(byte[] bArr, int i6) allocates a ResultCard and immediately calls RecognizeCardData — no byte[] length cap, no format check, no content validation. The three async paths in the Java call graph (nd.k::run, camcard.d::doInBackground, ob.g2::run) all originate from user-initiated scan flows and are production-reachable.

Step 5 — Function pointer resolution for PTR_recognize_00682c08. The dispatch target is runtime-resolved and cannot be confirmed statically from libbcr.so. However, the ART crash trace is informative: GetStaticMethodID is invoked from inside the recognize implementation, which means recognize calls back into the Java runtime using a class name or method descriptor. The fault in Class::GetDescriptor indicates that the class object pointer or descriptor string passed to FindMethodJNI is invalid. This is consistent with recognize using an _MsImage field (e.g., an internal string pointer or an index into a class name table) to construct or select the JNI callback target, and that field having been corrupted by the malformed input byte[].

Step 6 — Classification. The taint path is: attacker byte[] → MsDecodeBuffer (no validation) → _MsImage with attacker-influenced fields → recognize (opaque, complex OCR engine) → GetStaticMethodID with potentially attacker-influenced descriptor → crash in ART Class::GetDescriptor. This is not a harness-only failure; GetStaticMethodID is a legitimate ART operation invoked by the app's own native code on a code path reachable from camera/gallery input. The crash indicates corrupted or invalid state propagated from attacker-controlled image data. I classify this as a genuine vulnerability with confidence 0.62 — the taint path is clear but the exact _MsImage field that flows into the JNI descriptor cannot be confirmed without the full recognize implementation.
reasons: Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData passes the MsDecodeBuffer-produced _MsImage directly to recognize without validating any of its fields; attacker-influenced bytes can corrupt _MsImage internals that recognize subsequently uses to construct a JNI callback.

recognize is an opaque indirect call (PTR_recognize_00682c08) that invokes GetStaticMethodID back into ART; the crash in Class::GetDescriptor indicates the class or method descriptor used in that call is invalid, consistent with corrupted _MsImage state derived from the malformed input.

All three Java call graph paths reaching RecognizeCardData originate from production user-initiated scan flows (camera, gallery, async tasks), making this vulnerability realistically triggerable without harness involvement.

evidence.note: _MsImage* local_60 is produced by MsDecodeBuffer from an unvalidated attacker-controlled byte[] and passed directly to recognize; no structural validation (dimensions, data pointer, internal string fields) is applied between decode and recognition.

Dispatch thunk; implementation is runtime-resolved. The crash in ART Class::GetDescriptor originates from a GetStaticMethodID call inside this implementation, indicating the resolved recognize logic uses an _MsImage-derived value as a class or method descriptor that is invalid under malformed input.

ART crash trace triggered by a GetStaticMethodID call from inside recognize; the fault in Class::GetDescriptor indicates the class object reference or descriptor string is corrupt, consistent with attacker-influenced _MsImage fields propagated through the recognize logic.

recommendations: After MsDecodeBuffer returns, validate all _MsImage structural fields (non-NULL data pointer, width and height within expected bounds, valid stride) before passing the structure to recognize; abort processing on any out-of-range value.

Resolve and cache all JNI method IDs at library load time in JNI_OnLoad using compile-time constant descriptor strings; never derive class or method descriptor strings from image-decoded data or _MsImage fields at runtime.
Obtain the full implementation behind PTR_recognize_00682c08 and audit every _MsImage field access for use as a length, index, or string pointer in JNI calls or memory operations.
assumptions: MsDecodeBuffer populates _MsImage with fields derived from the attacker-controlled byte[]; at least one of those fields is subsequently used by recognize to construct or select a JNI class/method descriptor string.
The crash in Class::GetDescriptor represents illegal native state introduced by attacker-controlled image data, not a benign ART class-initialization race or harness artifact.
The ResultCard object and int mode flag do not constrain the decoding or recognition logic in a way that prevents malformed _MsImage construction.
limitations: The implementation behind PTR_recognize_00682c08 is runtime-resolved and not statically visible; the precise mechanism by which a corrupted _MsImage field reaches the GetStaticMethodID descriptor cannot be confirmed without dynamic analysis or the full SO binary.
The _MsImage struct layout and field semantics are unknown; it is not determinable which specific field flows into the JNI callback descriptor.
No sanitizer output (ASAN/HWASAN) is present; the exact memory violation type and faulting address are unconfirmed.

exploit.trigger_method: Crafted image byte[] that causes MsDecodeBuffer to produce a malformed _MsImage, whose fields are used by recognize in a GetStaticMethodID callback into ART
prerequisites: Attacker can supply image data to the app via camera, gallery, or an intent pointing to an attacker-controlled file.
Target device runs the vulnerable version of libbcr.so loaded by com.intsig.BCRLite.
No validation of _MsImage fields is applied between MsDecodeBuffer and recognize in the deployed binary.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an image byte[] that causes MsDecodeBuffer to return success (iVar3 == 0) but populate _MsImage with attacker-influenced field values (e.g., a corrupted internal string pointer or an index that maps to an attacker-controlled class name).
Deliver the crafted image to CamCard through a realistic scan flow (camera scan activity, gallery import, or file-sharing intent) so that RecognizeCard is invoked with the payload.
recognize processes the malformed _MsImage and uses a derived field in a GetStaticMethodID call, passing an invalid class descriptor to ART, crashing in Class::GetDescriptor.

With precise control over which _MsImage field feeds the descriptor, an attacker may be able to redirect the JNI lookup to an attacker-chosen class, potentially enabling further JNI abuse or information disclosure.
Full exploitation beyond DoS requires reversing PTR_recognize_00682c08 to confirm which _MsImage field flows into the descriptor and the degree of attacker control over its value.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.intsig.BCRLite/<ScanActivity> --es image_path "/sdcard/Download/payload_recognize.img"
exploit.notes: Activity name and exact image format are placeholders pending AndroidManifest review and full decompilation of PTR_recognize_00682c08. Exploitability beyond reliable DoS is theoretical until the _MsImage field to JNI descriptor data-flow is confirmed via dynamic instrumentation.
